title: Neural Network Training
description: Weight initialization, loss functions, optimizers, normalization, regularization, LR schedules, and production-scale training — with FAANG interview depth on every component.
details: Training modern neural networks requires orchestrating initialization, loss functions, optimizers, normalization, regularization, and LR schedules. Each component was designed to solve a specific failure mode: vanishing gradients (He init, residual connections, BatchNorm), optimization instability (Adam, warmup), or generalization (dropout, weight decay). Production training adds distributed data parallel (DDP), mixed precision, and checkpointing.

sections.title: Weight Initialization — Breaking Symmetry
sections.description:
Why Initialization Matters

If all weights start at zero, every neuron computes the same output, every gradient is identical, and symmetry is never broken — the network learns nothing. Initialization must provide the right variance scaling so signals and gradients don't vanish or explode with depth.

Initialization strategies:

Xavier/Glorot (sigmoid, tanh):
  W ∼ U[−√(6/(nᵢₙ+nₒᵤₜ)), +√(6/(nᵢₙ+nₒᵤₜ))]
  Var[W] = 2 / (fan_in + fan_out)
  → Keeps activation variance constant forward, gradient variance constant backward

He/Kaiming (ReLU, Leaky ReLU):
  W ∼ N(0, √(2/nᵢₙ))
  Var[W] = 2 / fan_in
  → Doubles Xavier variance because ReLU zeros out ~50% of activations

Orthogonal (RNNs):
  W = random orthogonal matrix (WᵀW = I)
  → Prevents vanishing/exploding in recurrent dynamics

[Intuition]

"He init doubles the variance of Xavier because the ReLU zeros out half the activations — you need to compensate." If you use Xavier with ReLU, the effective variance is halved, so deeper layers get weaker signals.

[Interview Angle] FAANG Insight

Q: "You initialized a ReLU network with Xavier init and it's not training. Why?"
A: Xavier assumes the activation is linear/sigmoid with full gradient flow. ReLU zeros 50% of activations, so the effective variance drops — signal shrinks with depth. Solution: use He init (Var = 2/fan_in) which compensates by doubling the variance.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Choosing Initialization: For ReLU networks, always use He init (var=2/fan_in). For tanh/sigmoid, use Xavier. For RNNs, use orthogonal. If training is unstable, check that init variance matches the activation function. Many training failures trace back to wrong init.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Xavier/Glorot init (for tanh, sigmoid)
tf.keras.initializers.GlorotUniform()
tf.keras.initializers.GlorotNormal()

# He init (for ReLU, leaky ReLU)
tf.keras.initializers.HeUniform()
tf.keras.initializers.HeNormal()

# Usage in layers:
layer = tf.keras.layers.Dense(256, activation='relu',
    kernel_initializer='he_normal')

# Orthogonal init (for RNNs):
tf.keras.initializers.Orthogonal()

sections.title: Loss Functions — Regression
sections.description:
Comparing Regression Losses

MSE (L2):   L = (y − ŷ)²          ← grad ∝ (y−ŷ), ∀y
MAE (L1):   L = |y − ŷ|          ← grad = ±1, constant
Huber:     L = ½(y−ŷ)² if |δ|<1 else |y−ŷ|−½  ← hybrid
Smooth L1: Same as Huber, used in Faster R-CNN

[Intuition]

MSE penalizes large errors quadratically (sensitive to outliers). MAE penalizes all errors linearly (robust, but slow convergence). Huber combines both: L2 near 0 for smooth convergence, L1 far from 0 for outlier robustness. Smooth L1 is identical to Huber — used in object detection because bounding box regression needs outlier robustness.

When to Use

MSE: No outliers, normal residuals, or maximum likelihood under Gaussian noise
MAE: Outliers present, or when median prediction is preferred (MAE minimizes median)
Huber/Smooth L1: Default for regression, especially with noisy data or object detection

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Choosing Regression Loss: MSE is the default but is sensitive to outliers. If your data has noise/outliers, use Huber or Smooth L1. For robust estimation, MAE gives median (not mean). For object detection, Smooth L1 is standard.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# MSE
mse = tf.keras.losses.MeanSquaredError()

# MAE / L1
mae = tf.keras.losses.MeanAbsoluteError()

# Huber (delta parameter controls L1/L2 boundary)
huber = tf.keras.losses.Huber(delta=1.0)

# Smooth L1 (for object detection bounding box regression)
def smooth_l1(y_true, y_pred):
    diff = tf.abs(y_true - y_pred)
    return tf.where(diff < 1, 0.5 * diff**2, diff - 0.5)

sections.title: Loss Functions — Classification
sections.description:
Binary & Categorical Cross-Entropy

BCE:   L = −[y·log(p) + (1−y)·log(1−p)]     ← binary {0,1}
CCE:   L = −Σᵢ yᵢ·log(pᵢ)                     ← multi-class
Focal: L = −(1−pₜ)ᵞ·log(pₜ)                     ← γ=2, down-weights easy examples

Label Smoothing: Replace hard targets y ∈ {0,1} with y_smooth = (1−ε)·y + ε/K. Prevents overconfidence, improves calibration. Used in Transformer training.

[Intuition]

"Label smoothing prevents the model from assigning 100% probability to any class — this improves calibration and generalization." Focal Loss down-weights the loss contribution of well-classified examples (pₜ > 0.5), forcing the model to focus on hard examples — critical for class imbalance.

[Concrete Numbers]

For an easy example (pₜ = 0.9): CE = 0.105, Focal (γ=2) = 0.001 — 100× less loss.
For a hard example (pₜ = 0.3): CE = 1.204, Focal (γ=2) = 0.589 — only 2× less loss.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Classification Loss Selection: Cross-entropy is the default for most tasks. Use Focal loss for class imbalance (γ=2 down-weights easy examples by 4× vs CE). Use label smoothing (ε=0.1) for Transformers to prevent overconfidence.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Binary cross-entropy (binary classification)
bce = tf.keras.losses.BinaryCrossentropy()

# Categorical cross-entropy (multi-class, one-hot labels)
cce = tf.keras.losses.CategoricalCrossentropy()

# Sparse CCE (multi-class, integer labels)
sce = tf.keras.losses.SparseCategoricalCrossentropy()

# With label smoothing (ϵ=0.1):
cce_smooth = tf.keras.losses.CategoricalCrossentropy(label_smoothing=0.1)

# Manual Focal Loss:
def focal_loss(gamma=2.0, alpha=0.25):
    def loss(y_true, y_pred):
        ce = -y_true * tf.math.log(y_pred + 1e-7)
        weight = (1 - y_pred) ** gamma  # down-weight easy
        return tf.reduce_mean(alpha * weight * ce)
    return loss

sections.title: Advanced Losses — Contrastive & Triplet
sections.description:
Learning Embedding Spaces

Contrastive (pairs):
  L = ½y·D² + ½(1−y)·max(m−D, 0)²
  y=1 (similar pair) → minimize D²  |  y=0 (dissimilar) → push apart by margin m

Triplet (anchor, positive, negative):
  L = max(d(a,p) − d(a,n) + margin, 0)
  → anchor-positive closer, anchor-negative farther by at least margin

InfoNCE / NT-Xent (SimCLR):
  L = −log(exp(sim(i,j)/τ) / Σₖ exp(sim(i,k)/τ))
  → contrastive at batch level, uses temperature τ

[Intuition]

"Learn an embedding space where similar examples are close and dissimilar are far." The key in Triplet Loss is mining — using semi-hard negatives (negatives that are closer to the anchor than the positive but still outside the margin). Random negatives are too easy; hard negatives cause collapse.

InfoNCE Temperature τ

Lower τ (e.g., 0.07) → sharper distribution → focuses on hardest negatives. Higher τ (e.g., 1.0) → smoother → includes more negatives. SimCLR uses τ=0.5.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Contrastive Loss Tuning: Triplet loss needs careful margin tuning (start with 0.2). Hard negative mining is critical — random negatives are too easy. Use a margin that separates positive from negative pairs by at least the margin distance.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Triplet loss (anchor, positive, negative)
def triplet_loss(anchor, positive, negative, margin=0.2):
    pos_dist = tf.reduce_sum(tf.square(anchor - positive), axis=1)
    neg_dist = tf.reduce_sum(tf.square(anchor - negative), axis=1)
    return tf.reduce_mean(tf.maximum(pos_dist - neg_dist + margin, 0))

# Contrastive loss (pairs)
def contrastive_loss(y_true, distances, margin=1.0):
    # y_true: 1 = similar, 0 = dissimilar
    similar = y_true * distances**2
    dissimilar = (1 - y_true) * tf.maximum(margin - distances, 0)**2
    return tf.reduce_mean(similar + dissimilar) / 2

sections.title: Optimizers — SGD to AdamW to Lion
sections.description:
Evolution of Optimization

SGD:      wₜ₊₁ = wₜ − η·gₜ              ← simple, generalizes well
Momentum: v = α·v − η·g ; w += v        ← damped oscillation
NAG:      look ahead: g(w−α·v)             ← smarter momentum
AdaGrad:  r = r + g² ; w = w − η·g/√r    ← per-param LR, r grows → LR→0
RMSprop:  r = β·r + (1−β)·g² ; w = −η·g/√r  ← EMA prevents LR→0
Adam:     m = β₁m + (1−β₁)g               ← first moment (mean)
          v = β₂v + (1−β₂)g²              ← second moment (uncentered var)
          w = w − η·m̂/(√v̂ + ε)            ← m̂,v̂ = bias-corrected
AdamW:    w = w − η·(m̂/(√v̂+ε) + λw)      ← decoupled weight decay
Lion:     w = w − η·sign(β₁m + (1−β₁)g)  ← sign-based, auto-discovered

[Key] FAANG Insight

Q: "Why does Adam need bias correction?"
A: mₜ and vₜ are initialized as 0 vectors. At early steps, the estimates are biased toward 0 (especially vₜ, because β₂ is close to 1). Bias correction divides mₜ by (1-β₁ᵗ) and vₜ by (1-β₂ᵗ), effectively scaling up the early estimates. Without correction, the first steps have very large effective learning rates (because v̂ is tiny).

[Concrete Numbers]

Default Adam: β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-7. At step t=1: v₁ = 0.999·0 + 0.001·g² = 0.001·g². v̂₁ = 0.001·g²/(1−0.999¹) = 0.001·g²/0.001 = g². So the correction divides out the (1-β₂) factor — without correction, √v₁ = √(0.001·g²) ≈ g·0.032, making the effective step 31× larger than intended.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Optimizer Selection: AdamW is the default for LLMs and large models. Use SGD with momentum for smaller models (<10M params) where generalization matters. Warmup + cosine decay is the standard LR schedule for all optimizers at scale.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# SGD
sgd = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01)
sgd_momentum = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01, momentum=0.9)
sgd_nesterov = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01, momentum=0.9, nesterov=True)

# Adam
adam = tf.keras.optimizers.Adam(learning_rate=0.001)

# AdamW
adamw = tf.keras.optimizers.AdamW(
    learning_rate=0.001, weight_decay=0.01)

# RMSprop
rmsprop = tf.keras.optimizers.RMSprop(learning_rate=0.001)

# Lion (experimental, via optax or manual)
# lion update: w = w - lr * sign(beta1 * m + (1 - beta1) * g)

sections.title: Dropout & Normalization
sections.description:
Inverted Dropout

During training:  h_mask = h · Bernoulli(p) / p    ← scale by 1/p
During inference: h_mask = h                        ← no scaling

The scaling by 1/p during training ensures the expected activation magnitude is the same at train and test time. Without this, you'd need to scale at inference (not inverted).

Normalization Types

BatchNorm (Ioffe 2015):   y = γ·(x−μᵦ)/σᵦ + β ← across batch
LayerNorm (Ba 2016):     y = γ·(x−μℓ)/σℓ + β ← across features (per sample)
RMSNorm (Zhang 2019):    y = γ·x/RMS(x)     ← re-centering removed, simpler

[Intuition]

"BatchNorm fixes gradient flow through deep networks by keeping activations in the linear regime. LayerNorm is needed for Transformers because batch size varies (but sequence length is fixed)." The key difference: BatchNorm normalizes across the batch (per-channel statistics), which means it behaves differently at train vs inference (uses running averages). LayerNorm normalizes across features (per-sample), which is deterministic at any batch size.

Why Transformers use LayerNorm

Variable-length sequences → batch statistics across different-length sequences are ill-defined. LayerNorm treats each token independently, making it batch-size agnostic. Modern LLMs use pre-norm (normalize before sublayer, not after) for better gradient flow.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Normalization Choice: Use LayerNorm for Transformers, RNNs, and any sequence model. Use BatchNorm for CNNs with batch sizes ≥16. Use RMSNorm (LLaMA, Mistral) when you want ~15% faster LayerNorm with equivalent quality.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Dropout (rate = fraction of units to drop)
model.add(tf.keras.layers.Dropout(rate=0.5))

# Batch Normalization
model.add(tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization())

# Layer Normalization
model.add(tf.keras.layers.LayerNormalization(epsilon=1e-6))

# Pre-norm Transformer block pattern:
# x = x + sublayer(norm(x))  ← norm BEFORE sublayer

# Important: BatchNorm should NOT be combined with Dropout
# where you might place it before the nonlinearity

sections.title: Regularization & Early Stopping
sections.description:
Regularization Techniques

L2 (Weight Decay):    L_reg = λ·||w||²/2      ← gradient: λ·w
L1 (Lasso):          L_reg = λ·||w||₁         ← induces sparsity
Early Stopping:      Stop when val loss plateaus (patience=5, restore_best_weights)
Stochastic Depth:    Randomly skip entire layers during training (for very deep nets)

[Concrete Numbers]

ResNet-50 on ImageNet: LR=0.1, weight decay=1e-4, batch size=256, momentum=0.9, trained for 90 epochs. Weight decay 1e-4 means a weight of magnitude 10 gets a gradient penalty of 0.001 — barely visible but critical for generalization.

[Intuition]

Weight decay at λ=1e-4 means: each update shrinks weights by 0.01% of their magnitude. Over 100K steps, this prevents weights from growing unbounded — it implicitly constrains the model complexity. L1 goes further: the constant gradient (λ) pushes small weights to exactly 0 (sparsity).

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Regularization Strategy: Start with weight decay (λ=0.01–0.1) and early stopping. Add dropout (p=0.1–0.5) if overfitting persists. For massive models, stochastic depth (drop entire layers) is more effective than dropout.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# L1/L2 regularization on a layer
layer = tf.keras.layers.Dense(
    64,
    activation='relu',
    kernel_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L2(l2=0.0001),
    bias_regularizer=tf.keras.regularizers.L1(l1=0.0)
)

# Early stopping callback
early_stop = tf.keras.callbacks.EarlyStopping(
    monitor='val_loss',
    patience=5,              # epochs to wait before stopping
    restore_best_weights=True  # revert to best checkpoint
)

# Usage:
# model.fit(X, y, validation_split=0.2,
#           callbacks=[early_stop])

sections.title: Learning Rate Schedules
sections.description:
LR Schedule Comparison

Step Decay:      η = η₀ × 0.1 every 30 epochs     ← simple, needs tuning
Cosine Decay:   η = η₀ × ½(1+cos(π·t/T))         ← smooth, no extra hyperparams
Warmup:         η = η₀ · t/t_warmup              ← linear 0→LR over t_warmup steps
Cosine + Warmup: η = warmup then cosine            ← standard LLM practice
OneCycle:       warmup → cosine to maxLR → anneal  ← quick convergence

[Intuition]

"Warmup is critical for Adam because the moving averages are initialized at 0 — the first few steps have very large effective LRs without warmup." At step 1, v̂ is tiny (because v₁ = (1−β₂)g² ≈ 0.001g²), so the step is huge. Warmup lets the optimizer "warm up" its statistics before seeing high learning rates.

[Concrete Numbers] LLM Training Recipe

LLaMA 3: Cosine schedule with 2,000 warmup steps, peak LR=3×10⁻⁴, min LR=3×10⁻⁵. Training for 15T tokens with ~10K steps to peak LR.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — LR Schedule Selection: For most tasks, cosine decay with linear warmup (5–10% of total steps) is the safest choice. Step decay requires knowing when loss plateaus — use cosine to avoid tuning. OneCycle is great for fast convergence in limited-budget training.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Step decay callback
def step_decay(epoch):
    initial_lr = 0.001
    drop = 0.1
    epochs_drop = 30.0
    return initial_lr * (drop ** (epoch // epochs_drop))

lr_scheduler = tf.keras.callbacks.LearningRateScheduler(step_decay)

# Cosine decay
cosine_lr = tf.keras.optimizers.schedules.CosineDecay(
    initial_learning_rate=0.001,
    decay_steps=100000,
    alpha=0.0  # decay to 0
)

# Cosine decay with warmup
def cosine_with_warmup(step, warmup_steps=2000, peak_lr=0.001):
    if step < warmup_steps:
        return peak_lr * step / warmup_steps
    progress = (step - warmup_steps) / (100000 - warmup_steps)
    return peak_lr * 0.5 * (1 + tf.cos(np.pi * progress))

# OneCycle scheduler popularized by fast.ai

sections.title: Training at Production Scale — DDP, FSDP, Mixed Precision
sections.description:
Distributed Training Strategies

DDP (Data Distributed Parallel):
  Each GPU has full model copy, processes different micro-batches
  Gradients sync via all-reduce after each step
  Limitation: each GPU must fit the entire model + optimizer states

FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel):
  Shard optimizer states, gradients, AND parameters across GPUs
  Only materialize what's needed for current computation (fwd/bwd)
  ZeRO-1: optimizer states only | ZeRO-2: + gradients | ZeRO-3: + params
  ZeRO-3 enables training 70B models on 8×A100 (vs 80×A100 needed for DDP)

Gradient Accumulation:
  Run N forward/backward passes before one optimizer step
  Effective batch size = per_gpu_batch × num_gpus × accumulation_steps

Mixed Precision Training

FP16:  2 bytes/param. Pros: 2× speed, halved memory. Cons: tiny dynamic range
BF16:  2 bytes/param. Same range as FP32, less precision. No underflow issues
FP32:  4 bytes/param. Full precision, 2× memory of FP16/BF16

Mixed precision: master weights in FP32, forward/backward in FP16 (or BF16). Loss scaling: multiply loss by a scale factor before backward (compensates for FP16 underflow), then divide gradients by scale before optimizer step. BF16 doesn't need loss scaling because its dynamic range matches FP32.

[Concrete Numbers]

LLaMA 3 70B at BF16: weights=140GB, Adam states=560GB (m+v=280GB each) → needs 7× H100 (80GB each) with FSDP ZeRO-3. With DDP: needs 7× more GPUs.

Checkpointing Strategy

Save: model weights, optimizer state (m, v for Adam), LR scheduler state, epoch/step counter, random seed state. Use tf.train.Checkpoint or PyTorch torch.save. Without optimizer state, training cannot be resumed exactly.

[Interview Angle] Debugging NaN Loss

Set TF_CPP_MIN_LOG_LEVEL=0 for verbose logging. Use tf.debugging.check_numerics to identify the first layer producing NaN. Common causes: learning rate too high, gradient explosion, division by zero (e.g., BatchNorm with tiny batch). The overfit-one-batch test: if your model can't fit a single batch to 0 loss, the architecture can't learn the task.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow — Production Training: Start with gradient accumulation + DDP for models ≤7B parameters. Move to FSDP ZeRO-3 for larger models. Use BF16 mixed precision (no loss scaling needed). Always run the overfit-one-batch test before full training: if loss doesn't reach near-zero, the architecture or data pipeline is broken.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# TensorFlow distributed strategy
strategy = tf.distribute.MirroredStrategy()  # single-host DDP
# strategy = tf.distribute.MultiWorkerMirroredStrategy()  # multi-host

with strategy.scope():
    model = create_model()
    model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy')
    model.fit(dataset, epochs=10)

# Mixed precision policy (TF)
tf.keras.mixed_precision.set_global_policy('mixed_float16')

# Overfit-one-batch test:
#   batch = next(iter(dataset))
#   model.fit(batch[0], batch[1], epochs=100, verbose=0)
#   loss < 0.01 → architecture works
#   loss doesn't decrease → fundamental problem in architecture

# NaN debugging:
tf.debugging.check_numerics(tensor, "layer_name")

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A — Neural Network Training
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] FAANG Interview Questions

Q1: "Why does Adam need learning rate warmup?" — Meta E5, OpenAI L5

A: Adam maintains moving averages mₜ and vₜ. Both are initialized at 0, so early estimates are biased toward 0. Specifically, vₜ ≈ (1−β₂)Σ(β₂^(t−k))gₖ² ≈ tiny values in early steps. Without bias correction, the effective learning rate η/(√v̂+ε) is huge (potentially 100×+). Warmup starts with small η and increases to the target, allowing the optimizer's moment estimates to stabilize before seeing full learning rates.

Q2: "Compare LayerNorm and BatchNorm — when would you use each?" — Google L4

A: BatchNorm normalizes across the batch dimension (per-channel μ,σ over batch samples). It requires batch-dependent statistics, uses running averages at inference, and can be unstable at small batch sizes. LayerNorm normalizes across the feature dimension (per-sample μ,σ over all features). It's batch-size independent, deterministic at inference, and works for sequences of variable length. Use LayerNorm for Transformers/RNNs (sequence data), BatchNorm for CNNs (fixed-size inputs, large batches). Modern trend: LayerNorm is preferred for most architectures because it's simpler and batch-size independent.

Q3: "You're training a model and the loss is NaN. What do you check?" — Google L5

A: Systematic checklist: (1) Check learning rate — too high causes divergence. Reduce by 10×. (2) Check for division by zero — BatchNorm with batch size 1, epsilon too small. (3) Check data — do inputs/labels contain NaN or Inf? (4) Use gradient clipping — clip by global norm (max=1.0). (5) Enable NaN detection — tf.debugging.check_numerics pinpoints the first layer producing NaN. (6) Test with a single batch — if it overfits, the issue is in training dynamics, not architecture.

Q4: "Explain mixed precision training — why do you need loss scaling for FP16 but not BF16?" — NVIDIA L5

A: FP16 has a limited dynamic range: min positive normal = 6×10⁻⁸, max = 6.5×10⁴. Small gradient values underflow to 0. Loss scaling multiplies the loss before backward, shifting gradients into the representable range, then divides the optimizer update by the same scale. BF16 has the same 8-bit exponent as FP32 so its dynamic range matches FP32 (max ≈ 3×10³⁸) — small gradients never underflow. BF16 trades mantissa precision (7 bits vs FP16's 10 bits) for range. For LLM training, BF16 is preferred over FP16 because it eliminates loss scaling complexity.

Q5: "What's the difference between Adam and AdamW?" — Meta E5

A: In Adam, weight decay (L2 regularization) is coupled with the gradient update: w = w − η·(m̂/√v̂) − η·λ·w. Since the learning rate η multiplies the weight decay term, the effective regularization magnitude depends on η. AdamW decouples them: w = w − η·(m̂/√v̂) − η·λ·w (same equation!) — wait, the key insight is AdamW applies weight decay after the adaptive step, not as part of the gradient. The AdamW update is: w = w − η·(m̂/√v̂ + λ·w) vs Adam where weight decay is part of the loss gradient. This decoupling means the regularization doesn't interact with the adaptive learning rates, leading to better generalization, especially for large models.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• Kingma, D. & Ba, J. "Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization." — 2015
  • Loshchilov, I. & Hutter, F. "Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization." (AdamW) — 2019
  • Ioffe, S. & Szegedy, C. "Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift." — 2015
  • Ba, J., et al. "Layer Normalization." — 2016
  • He, K., et al. "Delving Deep into Rectifiers." (He Init) — 2015
  • Smith, L. "A disciplined approach to neural network hyper-parameters." (OneCycle) — 2018
  • Glorot, X. & Bengio, Y. "Understanding the difficulty of training deep feedforward neural networks." (Xavier Init) — 2010
  • Karpathy, A. "State of GPT" talk — 2023
  • Rajbhandari, S., et al. "ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models." — 2020
  • Micikevicius, P., et al. "Mixed Precision Training." — 2018
